repo_metadata.name: Xray-core
repo_metadata.description: Xray, Penetrates Everything. Also the best v2ray-core. Where the magic happens. An open platform for various uses.

first_match.snippet: // Int32Range deserializes from "1-2" or 1, so can deserialize from both int and number.
// windows binds an unbound udp socket to 0.0.0.0/[::] while first sendto
You can't just route 0.0.0.0/0 through xray0 interface, as that will result Xray-core itself try to reach its uplink thr
// But in fact it doesn't. So we need to wrap the listener to make it return 0.0.0.0(unspecified) as remote address.

dependabot_alerts.note: admin scope required; falling back to osv.dev

dependabot_config.raw_snippet:
# To get started with Dependabot version updates, you'll need to specify which
# package ecosystems to update and where the package manifests are located.
# Please see the documentation for all configuration options:
# https://help.github.com/github/administering-a-repository/configuration-options-for-dependency-updates

version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: "gomod"
    directory: "/" # Location of package manifests
    schedule:
      interval: "daily"
  - package-ecosystem: "github-action

prs.title: Loopback outbound: Avoid directly modifying potential shared ctx
DomainMatcher: Fix Match() result slice aliasing race
Direct/Freedom outbound: Block UDP responses that are come from `ipsBlocked` as well
Geodata: Support reversed CIDR rules in IP rules
header-custom finalmask: Extend expression primitives for 1:1 handshakes
Bump github.com/pires/go-proxyproto from 0.11.0 to 0.12.0
Direct/Freedom outbound: Add `ipsBlocked` (supports IP, CIDR, "geoip:", "ext:") and apply a default safe policy
Chore: Use buf.New() instead of buf.NewWithSize()
header-custom finalmask: Add UDP standalone handshake mode
Hysteria inbound: Use transport's authentication when there are no `clients`
Chore: Use buf.NewWithSize()
DNS: Log rule matches and client order when finalQuery returns early
DomainMatcher: Reduce startup time on Android as well
DomainMatcher: Fix CompactDomainMatcher rule indices
Bump golang.zx2c4.com/wireguard/windows from 0.5.3 to 0.6.1
Sniffing: Add `ipsExcluded` (supports IP, CIDR, "geoip:", "ext:")
Hysteria transport: Fix client-side clientManager
Sniffing: `domainsExcluded` supports "geosite:"
DomainMatcher: Reduce runtime memory usage and startup peak memory on iOS
header-custom finalmask: Add programmable handshake templates and runtime core
Global HTTP headers' masquerading: Add "curl"; Improve version generators
Routing: `process` supports UID on Android
Bump golang.org/x/net from 0.52.0 to 0.53.0
Bump actions/github-script from 8 to 9
TUN inbound: Add FreeBSD support
mKCP transport: Add `cwndMultiplier`; Apply unaggressive strategy by default
TUN inbound: Fix UDP FullCone NAT
TUN inbound: Add `gateway`, `dns`, `autoSystemRoutingTable`, `autoOutboundsInterface` for Windows
Bump google.golang.org/grpc from 1.79.3 to 1.80.0
WireGuard: Use Xray's buffer
Observatory: Clear removed outbounds
XDNS finalmask: Support `resolvers` (client) and `domains` (server) instead of `domain`
Hysteria inbound: Unwrap stats conn before extracting user
Finalmask `quicParams`: Add `bbrProfile` ("conservative" / "standard" (default) / "aggressive")
QUIC sniffer: Fix potential panic on malformed QUIC packets
TUN inbound: Closable by AlwaysOnInboundHandler
WireGuard outbound: Fix UDP FullCone NAT on Linux
Finalmask: Add `randRange` to "noise" (UDP), as the same as "header-custom"'s (TCP & UDP)
WireGuard inbound: Fix multi-peer; Fix potential routing issue
Bump nick-fields/retry from 3 to 4
VLESS Reverse Proxy: Add `sniffing` to outbound's `reverse` (which is actually an inbound)
Loopback outbound: Fix potential nil InboundFromContext
README.md: Add CELERITY to Web Panel
WireGuard: Implement UDP FullCone NAT
README.md: Add INCY to iOS & macOS Clients
Bump google.golang.org/grpc from 1.79.2 to 1.79.3
Xray-core: Refactor geodata
Finalmask: Refactor header conns to avoid multiple-copy; Add `randRange` to "header-custom" (TCP & UDP)
TUN inbound: Generate deterministic GUID on Windows
XHTTP transport: Some optimizations

entries.title: Governance single-point-of-failure on a privileged 37k-star network-proxy tool (C20)
entries.action_hint: Verify the release .dgst checksum yourself from an independent terminal before trusting any binary.
entries.threat_model_context: Xray-core is a network proxy — every installed instance routes user traffic (potentially including sensitive traffic) through the tool's VMess/VLESS/Shadowsocks/Reality/WireGuard protocol implementations. A malicious commit on main that modifies protocol handling, key derivation, or certificate handling reaches every user on the next release, and is binary-shipped across 60+ platform builds. The repository has NO upstream review gate: classic protection 404, zero rulesets, zero rules on default, no CODEOWNERS (E1). The formal-review rate is 2.3% (E2) — in practical terms, changes land on main and are released without a second human reviewing them. The anti-censorship context (E11) materially increases the plausibility of nation-state or APT-level adversaries targeting the publisher accounts.
entries.what_this_means: The supply-chain blast radius for Xray-core is large. A compromised maintainer account, a misreviewed PR, or a mistakenly merged malicious change reaches every user's traffic on the next release. There is no in-repo review gate to catch it.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: This is not evidence of current compromise. No open security issues, no published advisories, active OSSF indexing, and the .dgst checksum layer (F3) are all real mitigations. The finding is structural — it says the ONE gate that could catch malicious commits (code review) is effectively absent on this repo, not that an attack has happened.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: enable branch protection on main (ruleset OR classic) with required-approving-reviews=1, require status checks to pass, add CODEOWNERS covering security-sensitive directories (infra/conf, transport/internet, proxy/*). Consumer-side: verify .dgst from a separate OS/network than the one downloading the binary; pin a specific release tag you personally reviewed instead of tracking latest.

entries.title: 2.3% formal PR review + 7.7% any-review over 300-PR sample
entries.action_hint: Assume any recent commit on main is unreviewed; consumer pinning matters more here than with most tools.
entries.threat_model_context: 2.3% formal review and 7.7% any-review across the 300 most recent closed PRs (E2) means most changes reach main without a second set of eyes. Open PR #5640 is a defensive-hardening proposal that has been sitting 77 days with no engagement (E8); the pattern of security-relevant community PRs lingering is consistent with the overall review-rate number.
entries.what_this_means: External contributors who submit even security-flavored PRs should expect long review lags. Internal changes land without a peer review by default.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that the maintainer is acting maliciously. Xray's release cadence is very high (100 releases, latest 3 days ago — E3), so code moves fast — the speed is what necessitates the review gate, not an absence of care.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add at least one CODEOWNERS line so the security-sensitive directories auto-request review; enable a ruleset requiring ≥1 approving review. Consumer-side: read commit messages + diffs for the releases you install.

entries.title: Zero published GHSA advisories across 5.5 years despite heightened threat model
entries.action_hint: Monitor the repo's release notes directly for security language; don't assume a CVE feed will cover Xray issues.
entries.threat_model_context: SECURITY.md (E5) documents a private-disclosure channel and commits to releasing fixes before public discussion, but does NOT commit to publishing GHSA advisories after fixes ship. The repo has 0 published advisories over 5.5 years (E6). Possibilities: (a) no security-relevant bugs have been discovered — implausible for a 37k-star network-proxy tool; (b) fixes are shipped quietly in releases without formal advisory; (c) disclosure stops at the maintainer after the private-channel handoff. Either way, downstream consumers subscribing to the GitHub Security Advisory feed will see nothing, even for fixes that matter.
entries.what_this_means: There is no reliable machine-readable signal to a consumer that 'a security-relevant fix shipped in release X'. To know what was fixed, you must read the release notes.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that vulnerabilities are being suppressed. Silent-fix cadence is a documented upstream pattern (e.g. Linux kernel historically). Xray's fast release cadence is consistent with that — a fix ships in the next point release with no advisory. It's a disclosure-practice observation, not a security-hole observation.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: when a release includes a security-relevant fix, publish a GHSA advisory alongside. Consumer-side: monitor release notes directly; treat any Xray release as potentially a security patch.

entries.title: Binary integrity via per-asset .dgst checksums — no GPG / Sigstore / SLSA
entries.action_hint: Verify .dgst against SHA2-256 or SHA2-512 from an independent channel before running a binary on privileged hosts.
entries.threat_model_context: Every release asset has a matching .dgst file carrying MD5 + SHA1 + SHA2-256 + SHA2-512 (E4). SHA2-256 and SHA2-512 are cryptographically strong and sufficient for integrity. What is NOT present: GPG signatures, Sigstore bundles, SLSA provenance attestations, or any cross-signed attestation of the publisher identity. A compromised publisher account could re-upload both the binary and the .dgst, and downstream consumers who fetch both from GitHub would not detect it.
entries.what_this_means: If you download a release binary + .dgst from GitHub at the same time, verifying the checksum only tells you the pair is internally consistent — not that the pair is authentic. Out-of-band checksum comparison (e.g., a mirror, a third-party index) is the honest mitigation.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not 'no integrity check exists'. Strong hashes are present. This is a step below state-of-the-art for privileged tools (GPG / Sigstore / SLSA) but well above tools that ship no hashes at all.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: adopt Sigstore keyless signing (minimal operational burden, GitHub Actions integration available), publish SLSA Level 2+ provenance. Consumer-side: cross-check SHA2-256 against an independent mirror or distro packaging metadata.

entries.title: Dependabot + OSSF-Scorecard-indexed — mid-tier governance hygiene (5.1/10)
entries.action_hint: Non-blocking; confirms active dependency hygiene and automated CVE watching.
entries.threat_model_context: Dependabot (E9) is enabled on gomod + github-actions with daily polling — Go runtime dep CVEs are automatically surfaced to the maintainer. OSSF Scorecard (E7) is indexed at 5.1/10. Typical score detractors for a repo of this shape: Branch-Protection (we know this is 0), Signed-Releases (.dgst ≠ signature), Code-Review (low). Typical positives: Dependency-Update-Tool (dependabot), Pinned-Dependencies (via dependabot locking), SAST (not present here, would improve score).
entries.what_this_means: The governance gaps in F0/F1/F2 are real, but Xray is not a neglected repo. Automated dependency hygiene and OSSF indexing are active signals of operational investment.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: 5.1/10 is mid-tier, not low. It reflects specific fixable gaps (branch protection, code-review rate, signed releases), not a repo in disrepair.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: closing the branch-protection + CODEOWNERS + Signed-Releases gaps would materially move the OSSF score — estimated +2 to +3 points.

entries.title: Go ecosystem under-indexed — gomod parsing + release-asset inventory both missing
entries.action_hint: Supplement with `govulncheck ./...` on the installed source tree locally.
entries.threat_model_context: Two coverage gaps on this scan specific to Go: (1) harness does not parse go.mod/go.sum, so runtime dependency count is reported 0 and osv.dev total vulns is 0 (E12) — both coverage artifacts, not dep-free reality; (2) harness does not inventory release binary assets (the ~60 platform binaries per release), so the real distribution channel is reported as 0 channels.
entries.what_this_means: The absence of Go dep-CVE findings and the 'no channels detected' distribution picture are both scanner gaps, not properties of Xray.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that Xray's Go deps are clean or that it doesn't ship binaries. Both are facts the scanner didn't capture.
entries.how_to_fix: Scanner-side: add Go gomod parsing (ecosystem=Go is already recognized as a manifest; needs to populate runtime_count); add release-asset inventory (channel per platform). Consumer-side: run `govulncheck ./...` after cloning.

entries.claim: Branch protection is fully absent — no classic protection, no rulesets, no rules on default, no CODEOWNERS.

section_01.pill_row: 37.3k⭐ · MPL-2.0 · Go · 1 critical · 2 warnings · 3 info · CRITICAL
section_01.summary: Xray-core is a 37k-star Go network-proxy / VPN-tunneling / anti-censorship tool, very actively maintained (100 releases in 5.5 years, latest 3 days before scan). Governance posture drives a Critical verdict via C20: no branch protection, no rulesets, no CODEOWNERS on a privileged tool whose blast radius is every user's traffic. Review cadence is low (F1: 2.3% formal), and disclosure is silent (F2: 0 advisories in 5.5 years). Real mitigations exist: .dgst checksums per binary (F3), Dependabot on gomod (F4), OSSF-indexed at 5.1/10. The C20 call is structural — this scan finds no evidence of current compromise.

section_02.pill_row: F0 Critical · F1 Warning · F2 Warning · F3 Info · F4 Info · F5 Info

section_06.summary: One scorecard override on Q2: the V1.2.x-widened q2_oldest_open_security_item_age_days signal (25 total signals post-widening) correctly detected PR #5640 at 77 days open, firing red. Phase 4 softens to amber because the detected PR is a defensive-hardening proposal (not a fix for an exploitable vulnerability), 0 open security issues exist, and 100 releases show the fix-cadence pipeline works. override_reason = threshold_too_strict — first V1.2 scan to exercise this enum value; the signal's threshold is correct in principle but too aggressive when the security-keyword hit is hardening-without-fix-verb. Q1 red genuine (governance floor, no override). Q3 amber (SECURITY.md present, 0 advisories). Q4 amber (.dgst checksums present, no GPG/SLSA).

split_axis_decision.reasoning: Single consumer profile — every Xray user downloads a binary, runs it as a proxy / tunnel / VPN server, and routes traffic through it. No deployment-context variation (no 'inference-only' mode or client-vs-server split that changes the risk surface).

groups.headline: Critical — F0 drives the verdict via C20
groups.one_line_summary: Governance single-point-of-failure on a privileged network-proxy tool with 37k-star supply-chain reach.

items.claim: No branch protection / rulesets / CODEOWNERS on a 37k-star privileged tool that ships binaries to every install.

groups.headline: Warning — F1 + F2 describe the ongoing governance posture
groups.one_line_summary: Low review cadence + silent disclosure pattern.

items.claim: 2.3% formal PR review, 7.7% any-review across 300-PR sample. 77-day-old hardening PR sits unmerged.
Zero published GHSA advisories in 5.5 years. SECURITY.md commits to private disclosure but not to public advisory publication.

groups.headline: Info — F3 + F4 + F5 are supporting context
groups.one_line_summary: Positive mitigations and scanner coverage gaps.

items.claim: Binary integrity via .dgst (SHA2-256 + SHA2-512 per release). No GPG / Sigstore / SLSA — verify out-of-band.
Dependabot + OSSF-Scorecard-indexed (5.1/10). Active governance investment alongside the gaps in F0-F2.
Scanner coverage gaps: Go gomod parsing + release-asset inventory both missing.

selections.reason: Falsification (e.g., branch-protection ruleset enabled + CODEOWNERS added) collapses F0's C20 trigger and moves the verdict from Critical to Caution immediately.
If Xray begins publishing GHSA advisories retroactively for past fixes, F2 collapses to Info and the Q3 scorecard moves from amber to green — the silent-fix concern resolves.
If PR #5640 is merged (or closed with explanation), the 77-day security-keyword PR datapoint clears. Q2 advisory would fall out of red via the widened signal; the override rationale collapses.

entries.headline: Download the release binary for your platform from GitHub Releases
entries.non_technical: Xray ships prebuilt binaries for 60+ platforms. Use the one that matches your OS + architecture from the latest release on GitHub.

entries.headline: Verify the .dgst checksum before unzipping
entries.non_technical: Every release binary has a matching .dgst file with SHA2-256 and SHA2-512. Fetch it and verify against the binary. Ideally fetch the .dgst from a mirror or distro packaging metadata rather than GitHub to catch publisher-account compromise.
entries.command: curl -LO https://github.com/XTLS/Xray-core/releases/download/v26.4.17/Xray-linux-64.zip.dgst && (grep SHA2-256 Xray-linux-64.zip.dgst | awk '{print $2}' | xargs -I{} echo '{}  Xray-linux-64.zip') | sha256sum -c

entries.headline: Pin to a reviewed release tag in your deployment automation
entries.non_technical: Because Xray ships very fast (100 releases in 5.5 years, often within days of each other), track a specific tag you reviewed rather than 'latest'. Re-evaluate when the release-note changelog mentions security-relevant language.

entries.headline: Run govulncheck against Xray's Go module tree before running in a privileged context
entries.non_technical: The harness doesn't parse Go deps. Run govulncheck yourself on a clone of the pinned tag to surface any Go-runtime CVEs in the dep graph.
entries.command: git clone --depth 1 --branch v26.4.17 https://github.com/XTLS/Xray-core && cd Xray-core && go install golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest && govulncheck ./...

entries.headline: Run Xray as a non-root user with a restricted systemd unit
entries.non_technical: Xray only needs CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE to bind low ports + CAP_NET_ADMIN for TUN routing. Configure a systemd unit with exactly those capabilities rather than running as root. Limits blast radius if Xray is ever compromised.

executable_file_inventory.lead: Xray-core ships 60+ platform binaries per release (Windows / Linux / macOS / FreeBSD / OpenBSD / Android × architectures) plus a Docker image. Users install by downloading a release binary + .dgst checksum, or via `brew install xray`.
executable_file_inventory.layer1_summary: Primary surface is the compiled Go binary (main package in ./main). Repo also contains release/build workflow scripts and Dockerfile (the latter consumed by the release pipeline).

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: Network I/O, TLS termination, packet routing. Expected for a proxy tool; not vulnerabilities per se.
layer2_entries.notes: Privileged networking. Kernel-level reach on Linux when configured with TUN/TAP interfaces.

layer2_entries.network: Cross-compile Go → 60+ platforms; upload to GitHub Releases with .dgst hashing.
layer2_entries.notes: Release pipeline. Runs on every tag push.
Alpine-based Xray-core Docker image for the docker-publish.yml workflow.

executable_file_inventory.closing_note: Primary installation paths: (1) download release binary + .dgst checksum verification (recommended on privileged hosts), (2) `brew install xray` on macOS. No curl-pipe installer. No postinstall scripts.

pr_sample_review.intro: Representative rows from the 300-PR closed-and-merged sample. All 6 rows were merged by the lead maintainer (RPRX) without formal review — even concurrency fixes (#5959, #5960) and outbound-policy changes (#5957) shipped without a second reviewer. Full sample: 2.3% formal review, 7.7% any-review, 7 self-merges.

rows.note: CI + release + Docker + Dependabot + Copilot review
Low self-merge — most PRs from contributors
Harness didn't inventory the 60-binary release surface

rows.result: ⚠ Admin scope required (403); dependabot.yml present + active
⚠ 0 queries — harness does not parse go.mod/go.sum (F5)
⚠ 0 channels reported — 60+ GitHub Release binaries not inventoried (F5)
⚠ False positives: deserialization (2: both strings in error/comment), secrets (4: all test fixtures), tls_cors (18: CIDR 0.0.0.0/0 / 10.0.0.0/8 matches are network-routing config, not TLS/CORS)
⊗ Not available on this scanner host

entries: Go gomod parsing is not implemented — runtime_count=0 + osv total_vulns=0 are coverage artifacts, not Xray properties. F5 documents this.

Release-asset inventory is not captured — 60+ platform binaries per release (Windows / Linux / macOS / FreeBSD / OpenBSD / Android × architectures) are invisible to distribution_channels. F5 documents this.
Dependabot alerts API requires admin scope (HTTP 403); Xray HAS dependabot configured per dependabot.yml — automated dep-CVE alerting is present regardless.

Dangerous-primitives grep has false-positive hits on this Go codebase: deserialization hits are strings in comments/error messages, secrets hits are test fixtures, tls_cors hits include routable CIDRs (0.0.0.0/0 / 10.0.0.0/8) which are legitimate WireGuard config. The scanner's pattern-family regexes were tuned for web/Python/JS idioms and need Go-specific tightening.
Gitleaks secret-scanning not available on this scanner host — secret-in-history check deferred.
Anti-censorship threat model implies nation-state-capable adversaries; the scanner's default-baseline threat model does not escalate severity for such contexts. This scan applies default weighting.

does_anyone_check_the_code.short_answer: No — 2.3% formal review, no branch protection, no CODEOWNERS on a privileged tool

do_they_fix_problems_quickly.short_answer: Partly — 0 open security issues + 100 releases (3 days old), but a 77-day-old hardening PR sits unmerged
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.rationale: The Phase 3 advisory computed red via the V1.2.x-widened q2_oldest_open_security_item_age_days signal (77 days, from PR #5640). The signal correctly detected an open PR whose title matches security keywords — and fired red because 77 days is well above the 14-day threshold. But qualitative inspection (E8) shows PR #5640 is a defensive-hardening proposal ('Validate VMess outbound security settings — forbid insecure outbound settings'), not a fix for an exploitable vulnerability. There is no open security issue (E6: advisory_count=0, no open_security_issues), the repo has 100 releases with the latest 3 days old (E3), and the release cadence across the last 300 PRs is extremely fast. On the honest consumer-facing reading — 'when a problem is found, do fixes reach me quickly?' — the answer is closer to 'yes' than 'no'. Amber rather than green because the 77-day lag on a community hardening PR IS a real signal of thin review throughput; just not red-level.
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.edge_case: The widened signal can't yet distinguish 'security-keyword hardening PR' from 'CVE-labeled fix PR'. For a tool with 100 releases and zero open security issues, a solitary hardening-PR datapoint at 77 days overstates the fix-cadence problem. The threshold is correct in principle; its application to hardening-not-fix PRs is too strict in this case.
do_they_fix_problems_quickly.suggested_threshold_adjustment: Option A: widen the q2_oldest_open_security_item_age_days filter to match fix-verb keywords (fix, patch, address) alongside the security-noun keywords. Hardening PRs without fix-verbs would then fall out of Q2 and into Q1 (review-rate) instead. Option B: raise the red threshold from 14 to 30 days when open_security_issue_count is 0, keeping 14 days when an open issue exists.

do_they_tell_you_about_problems.short_answer: Partly — SECURITY.md with private channel, but 0 published advisories in 5.5 years

is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.short_answer: Partly — .dgst checksums (MD5+SHA1+SHA256+SHA512) on every release, but no GPG / Sigstore / SLSA

catalog_metadata.short_description: Go-based network proxy + VPN tunneling platform; supports VMess, VLESS, Shadowsocks, Trojan, WireGuard, Reality; anti-censorship focus.
catalog_metadata.target_user: Operator deploying an encrypted-tunnel proxy for personal or server traffic. Install is `brew install xray` on macOS or download prebuilt binary for 60+ platforms from GitHub Releases. Runs as a long-lived server process routing TCP/UDP.
catalog_metadata.shape: Go-CLI server daemon + release-binary distribution + network-proxy runtime (new shape — first Go-ecosystem entry in the catalog; first network-proxy + anti-censorship category).
catalog_metadata.prior_scan_note: None — first scan of Xray-core. Fourth wild V1.2-schema scan (after markitdown entry 15, ghostty entry 16, Kronos entry 17, kamal entry 18). First scan authored after V1.2.x signal widening landed in commit d803faf.

phase_4_structured_llm.timeline_intro: Xray-core's lifecycle — forked from v2ray-core in 2020, ships ~18 releases per year.
phase_4_structured_llm.evidence_intro: Command-backed evidence from the Phase 1 harness + direct gh api + .dgst checksum fetch.
phase_4_structured_llm.coverage_intro: Phase 1 harness coverage with 6 documented gaps (Go gomod parsing + release-asset inventory — both F5; dependabot alerts admin-scope; dangerous-primitives false positives on Go idioms; gitleaks unavailable; anti-censorship threat model weighting).

editorial_caption.text: Xray-core — 37k-star Go network proxy + anti-censorship tool (v2ray-core fork; VMess, VLESS, Shadowsocks, Trojan, WireGuard, REALITY). Critical verdict driven by C20: no branch protection, no rulesets, no CODEOWNERS, 2.3% formal PR review on a privileged tool whose blast radius is every user's traffic. Mitigations exist — .dgst per release, Dependabot on gomod, OSSF-indexed 5.1/10. Verify .dgst out-of-band, pin a reviewed tag.

body_paragraphs: Xray-core ships 60+ platform binaries per release (Windows / Linux / macOS / FreeBSD / OpenBSD / Android × architectures). Every copy of those binaries runs as a privileged network daemon in user environments — routing TCP/UDP traffic through VMess / VLESS / Shadowsocks / Trojan / Reality tunnels. A malicious commit on main that modifies how keys are derived, how TLS handshakes are completed, or how certificates are validated reaches every installed instance on the next release, and is binary-shipped from a single GitHub Actions pipeline across 60+ cross-compile targets.

The repository has no upstream review gate. `gh api repos/XTLS/Xray-core/branches/main/protection` returns 404. There are zero organization-level or repository-level rulesets. There are zero rules enforced on the default branch. There is no CODEOWNERS file at any of the four standard paths the harness checks. Formal-review rate across the last 300 closed PRs is 2.3% — in practice, the lead maintainer merges community PRs directly from main without a second human reviewer.

The C20 rule fires Critical here because the same repo that ships binaries to tens of thousands of consumers has no in-repo mechanism to catch a bad commit before it reaches a release. The anti-censorship context (E11) materially increases the plausibility of adversaries — this is exactly the kind of tool a nation-state or APT-level actor would target. That's the structural exposure, not current-compromise evidence; there's no indication any attack has happened. What there is, is no structural barrier to one if the publisher account is ever compromised.

What a consumer can actually do: verify the .dgst checksum against a mirror or distro-metadata source rather than just GitHub; pin to a specific release tag whose changelog you personally read; subscribe to release notifications and read each release's commits before upgrading.

Across the 300 most recent closed PRs, 2.3% carried a formal `APPROVED` review decision and 7.7% drew any reviewer engagement at all. Concurrency fixes (#5959 DomainMatcher race, #5960 loopback-outbound shared-ctx race) and outbound-policy changes (#5957) all merged without a second reviewer. The lead maintainer (RPRX, ~31% of top-contributor commits) is the merge actor on nearly everything.

PR #5640 is a community-submitted hardening proposal titled 'Validate VMess outbound security settings — forbid insecure outbound settings'. It has been open 77 days (created 2026-02-02) with its most recent activity 13 days before the scan. Not a fix for a specific vulnerability — a defensive hardening contribution. 77 days is the review-cadence signal, not the vulnerability signal.

Xray's release cadence is very fast — 100 releases in 5.5 years, the last three arriving within 2 days of each other — so code IS moving. The review gate is what's thin, not the throughput of shipping changes.

SECURITY.md exists and documents a private disclosure channel (the 'Report a vulnerability' button). It commits to releasing a fixed version before public discussion. What it does NOT commit to is publishing a GHSA advisory after the fix ships. The `security-advisories` API returns `count: 0` — zero published advisories across 5.5 years of active development on a 37k-star network-proxy tool.

Three readings are plausible: (a) no security-relevant issues have been discovered in 5.5 years — implausible for a codebase of this size handling this threat model; (b) security fixes are shipped quietly in point releases without formal GHSA publication — most likely, given the fast release cadence; (c) disclosure stops at the maintainer after private handoff. Reading (b) is a documented upstream pattern (the Linux kernel did this historically), but it means downstream consumers subscribing to the GitHub Security Advisory feed will see nothing, even for fixes that matter.

Consumer-side workaround: monitor release notes directly. Treat any Xray release as potentially security-relevant, read the commit range before upgrading, and don't rely on GHSA syndication to learn about fixes.

Every release asset has a matching `.dgst` file carrying MD5 + SHA1 + SHA2-256 + SHA2-512. SHA2-256 and SHA2-512 are cryptographically strong and sufficient for integrity as long as the checksum itself is trustworthy. What's absent: GPG signatures, Sigstore keyless attestations, SLSA provenance statements.

The gap this leaves: a compromise of the publisher account could re-upload both the binary AND the .dgst, and a downstream consumer who fetches both from GitHub would not detect the substitution. The .dgst layer protects against in-flight modification (MITM on the download) and accidental corruption. It does NOT protect against a compromise at the origin. That's why the action-steps include verifying .dgst from a separate channel — a mirror, a distro package manifest, the Homebrew formula — rather than trusting the GitHub-hosted pair as self-consistent.

Xray's operational posture is mid-tier rather than neglected. Dependabot is configured on both the `gomod` and `github-actions` ecosystems with daily polling — Go dep CVEs reach the maintainer automatically. OSSF Scorecard is indexed at 5.1/10 — the typical detractors for this shape (Branch-Protection=0, Code-Review=low, Signed-Releases=low) map exactly onto F0 and F3. Closing those gaps would meaningfully move the Scorecard number.
This is an Info-level finding because it describes existing positive investment, not a defect.

Two scanner-side coverage gaps on Go repos: (1) the harness detects go.mod + go.sum as manifests but doesn't parse them, so runtime_count=0 and osv.dev total_vulns=0 are scanner artifacts rather than a clean bill; (2) release-asset inventory isn't captured, so Xray's primary distribution channel (60+ platform binaries per release via GitHub Releases) is reported as 0 channels. Neither is an Xray property — both are harness gaps that a future V1.2.x patch could close.

For consumers reading this report: don't over-interpret 'no dep CVEs found' or 'no install channels detected'. Run `govulncheck ./...` against the Go module tree yourself after cloning; read the release binary inventory on the GitHub Releases page directly.
